---
title: "Kelvin Wong"
image: profile.jpg
about:
  template: trestles
  image-shape: round
  image-width: 15em
  links:
    - icon: github
      text: GitHub
      href: https://github.com/kelvin1189
    - icon: linkedin
      text: LinkedIn
      href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/[YOUR-LINKEDIN-SLUG]
---

## About me

I'm a senior specialist based in Taiwan working on life insurance risk management — valuation, ALM, and the modeling that underpins both. This blog is where I work through ideas adjacent to that work, usually with code, usually using public market data.

## About this blog

The blog opens with a multi-article series on the Dynamic Nelson-Siegel interest rate model under the IAIS Insurance Capital Standard, applied to TWD government bond data. That choice isn't accidental — it's a topic I'm thinking about in my current work, and writing about it publicly forces me to be explicit about my reasoning in a way that internal documents do not.

I want this site to function less as polished publication and more as a working notebook: a record of how I think through a problem, what I tried and discarded, where I'm still uncertain. Posts will sometimes be longer and more exploratory than typical technical blog posts as a result. The hope is that readers can see the actual decision points, not only the cleaned-up answer.

Everything published here is my own opinion and does not represent the views of any current or past employer.

## What this blog covers

The recurring threads here are:

- **Yield curve modeling** — Dynamic Nelson-Siegel, Vasicek, CIR, and other interest rate models
- **Insurance capital frameworks** — ICS, Solvency II, and scenario-based risk simulation
- **Quantitative risk modeling** — EWMA, GARCH, Kalman filtering, and related techniques used in valuation and risk
- **Paper notes** — short summaries of papers I've worked through, with reproductions where useful

## Background

**Education**

- M.Sc. in Risk Management and Insurance
- Undergraduate in Physics, with a double major in Mathematics

**Credentials**

- SOA candidate

**Experience**

- 3+ years in life insurance solvency and capital modeling
- 6+ years in risk management and capital modeling in P&C insurance
- 3 years as a graduate research assistant on a financial risk management and insurance project funded by Taiwan's National Science Council (now NSTC)

**Tools**

- 10+ years using R, Python, and SQL

## A note on AI assistance

English is my second language; my first is Chinese. Throughout the work on this blog I make active use of large language models — principally Claude (Anthropic), Gemini (Google), and Grok (xAI) — for English revision, code debugging, document structuring, and as a sparring partner when I want to stress-test an argument before committing it to a post.

The substantive content is mine: the choice of topics, the modeling decisions, the interpretation of results, and the conclusions. The AI tools help me express that content more clearly than I could on my own in a second language, and they sometimes catch errors I would have missed. Any errors that remain in published posts are also mine.

I mention this for transparency, and because the question of how to use these tools well in technical writing seems to me worth being open about rather than quietly working around.

## Get in touch

The fastest ways to reach me are GitHub (for code or blog corrections) or LinkedIn (for everything else). For technical errors in posts, opening a GitHub issue against the [blog repo](https://github.com/kelvin1189/actuarial-modeling-notes) is the most efficient route.
